name: Neoptolemus
description: Neoptolemus, also known as Pyrrhus, was the son of Achilles and Deidamia. He was a significant figure in the Trojan War after the death of his father. He was summoned to Troy by Odysseus, who revealed that the city could only be taken with the help of Achilles' son and the bow of Heracles, then possessed by Philoctetes. Neoptolemus was known for his fierce nature and uncompromising approach to war, contrasting with the more cunning strategies of Odysseus. He played a crucial role in the sack of Troy, killing Priam, the king of Troy, and sacrificing Polyxena on Achilles' tomb. After the war, he faced various adventures and ultimately met his death at Delphi, either due to a dispute with Orestes or at the instigation of the god Apollo. His character represents the brutal realities of war and the complex legacy of heroism.
subtitle: Achilles' son, the fierce warrior.

keyMyths.title: Summoned to Troy
keyMyths.description: Odysseus and Phoenix brought Neoptolemus to Troy after a prophecy revealed the city could not be taken without Achilles' son. He inherited his father's armor and fighting prowess, becoming a key figure in the war's final stages.

keyMyths.title: Sack of Troy
keyMyths.description: Neoptolemus was instrumental in the sack of Troy, displaying ruthless efficiency and killing King Priam at an altar. This act highlighted his aggressive nature and solidified his reputation as a formidable warrior.

keyMyths.title: Death at Delphi
keyMyths.description: Neoptolemus met his demise at Delphi, with conflicting accounts suggesting either a quarrel with Orestes or the machinations of Apollo. His death marked the end of a turbulent life marked by war and vengeance.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Provides the backdrop of the Trojan War and Achilles' legacy, influencing Neoptolemus's role.
sources.text: Provides the backdrop of the Trojan War and Achilles' legacy, influencing Neoptolemus's role.

sources.author: Sophocles
sources.description: Details Neoptolemus's involvement in retrieving Philoctetes and his bow.
